query: What are the requirements and fees required to submit a valid interconnection application for a large generator in ISO New England, based on the latest ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide and ISO-NE Tariff Schedule 22 Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)?
final_answer:
# ISO New England — Valid Interconnection Application Requirements & Fees for Large Generators (>20 MW)

Based on the latest ISO-NE Schedule 22 (Large Generator Interconnection Procedures, effective May 3, 2025, per ER25-2149-000), as reformed by FERC Order No. 2023 and Order No. 2023-A, the following summarizes the requirements and fees to submit a valid Interconnection Request (IR) for a large generating facility.

---

## 1. THRESHOLD ELIGIBILITY

Schedule 22 applies to **Large Generating Facilities (>20 MW)** interconnecting to the ISO-NE Administered Transmission System. [Source: ISO-NE OATT page](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/rules-procedures/oatt)

---

## 2. PRE-APPLICATION PREREQUISITES

Before submitting an IR, the Interconnection Customer must:

- Obtain an **ISO Customer ID**
- Obtain **Critical Energy Infrastructure Information (CEII) clearance**
- Register for an account in the **Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT)**

[Source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide/submit-an-interconnection-request)

---

## 3. TIMING: CLUSTER REQUEST WINDOW

Under Order No. 2023, IRs may **only** be submitted during a **45-Calendar-Day Cluster Request Window**. All IRs submitted and validated within the same window are considered **equally queued**. [Source: ISO-NE Order 2023 Compliance Overview, Sept. 2023](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf)

The **next Cluster Request Window** is expected to open **October 5, 2026 through November 19, 2026**. [Source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide)

---

## 4. COMPLETE INTERCONNECTION REQUEST PACKAGE (Section 3.4.2 of the LGIP)

To be deemed a **valid Interconnection Request**, the following must be submitted during the Cluster Request Window:

### 4A. Appendix 1 Interconnection Request Form

The completed form (Appendix 1 to the LGIP) must include:

| Element | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Project type** | New Large Generating Facility, Material Modification, or NR→CNR change |
| **Interconnection Service election** | Network Resource (NRIS — energy only) or Capacity Network Resource (CNRIS — energy + capacity) |
| **Facility location** | Address, Town/City, County, State |
| **Requested Point of Interconnection (POI)** | Definitive POI required |
| **Generating Facility type & fuel** | Including electric storage specifications if applicable |
| **Capacity data** | Gross/Net MW at four temperature benchmarks (≥0°F, ≥20°F, ≥50°F, ≥90°F) |
| **Requested service levels (MW)** | CNR Capability Summer/Winter; NR Capability Summer/Winter |
| **Equipment configuration** | Number of units, GSUs, control technologies |
| **Key dates** | Commercial Operation Date, Initial Synchronization Date, In-Service Date |
| **Customer information** | Company name, ISO Customer ID#, authorized signature |

[Source: ISO-NE Appendix 1 Interconnection Request form](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)

### 4B. Technical Data & Models

All technical data, models (PSS/E, EMT as applicable), and attachments specified in Appendix 1 must be submitted with the IR. ISO recommends submitting **at least 15 business days before the close of the CRW** to allow time for review and curing of deficiencies. [Source: ISO-NE Order 2023 FAQ, Jan. 2024](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/2024_01_18_tc_order2023_new_process_faq_final.pdf)

### 4C. Site Control

| Service Type | Requirement |
|---|---|
| **CNR Interconnection Service** | **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name |
| **NR Interconnection Service** | **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name; **OR, in lieu thereof:** (i) a **$10,000/MW deposit** (min $500,000 / max $2,000,000), (ii) a signed officer's affidavit stating Site Control is unobtainable due to Regulatory Limitations, and (iii) documentation explaining the Regulatory Limitations |
| **Modification to existing facility** | Customer certifies it already has Site Control and no additional real property is required |

*Note: The Order No. 2023 compliance design references 90% site control at the IR stage (per the presentation slides), while the currently posted Appendix 1 form continues to reference 100% for CNR. The tariff itself (Section 3.4.2) as of the latest LGIP effective date (May 3, 2025) should be consulted for the binding requirement.*

[Source: Appendix 1 form; ISO-NE Dec. 2023 TC presentation](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100006/a06_12212023_order_2023_compliance_december_tc_summary_presentation.pdf)

---

## 5. FEES & DEPOSITS — SUMMARY TABLE

All deposits to the System Operator must be paid in **cash via electronic transfer** (study deposits and initial deposit), while the Commercial Readiness Deposit may be in the form of **cash or an irrevocable Letter of Credit** from an ISO-NE-approved financial institution. [Source: ISO-NE Dec. 2023 TC presentation](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100006/a06_12212023_order_2023_compliance_december_tc_summary_presentation.pdf)

| Fee/Deposit | Amount (New NR/CNR) | Amount (NR→CNR Change) | Refundable? | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Deposit** | **$50,000** | **$50,000** | Potentially non-refundable | Applied toward ISO costs for processing the IR, developing study agreements, and developing the LGIA |
| **Study Deposit** | **$250,000** | **$100,000** | Refundable (unspent portion) | Applied toward Cluster Study costs |
| **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)** | **$500,000** (2× study deposit) | **$200,000** (2× study deposit) | Refunded per Section 3.7 upon meeting milestones | Demonstrates commercial readiness |
| **Site Control Alternative Deposit** (NR only) | **$10,000/MW** (min $500K, max $2M) | N/A | Refundable within cure period | In lieu of Site Control for NR, when Regulatory Limitations exist |

**Total upfront at IR submission (new CNR/NR): $800,000** (initial $50K + study $250K + CRD $500K)

[Source: ISO-NE Jan. 2024 TC presentation, LGIP Section 3.4.1.1 redlines](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf)

---

## 6. SUBSEQUENT FINANCIAL COMMITMENTS THROUGH THE PROCESS

| Stage | Trigger | Financial Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Post-Cluster Study Report** | Within 20 CD of Cluster Study Report Meeting | Additional CRD = **5% of assigned Network Upgrade costs** |
| **Facilities Study Entry** | Execution of Facilities Study Agreement | CRD = **10% of Network Upgrade costs**, plus **greater of 25% of estimated FS cost or $250,000**, plus proof of **100% Site Control** |
| **LGIA Execution** | Upon signing the Interconnection Agreement | **20% deposit** (less readiness deposits already on hand) |

[Sources: ISO-NE Oct. 2023 Cluster Study Cycle presentation; Appendix 3 (Facilities Study Agreement)](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100004/a03b_2023_10_17_tc_order2023_proposed_compliance_overview.pdf)

---

## 7. WITHDRAWAL PENALTIES

Under Order No. 2023, withdrawal penalties apply if an Interconnection Customer withdraws after certain milestones. The Commercial Readiness Deposit is at risk if the customer withdraws without meeting the applicable milestone. [Source: ISO-NE Order 2023 Compliance Overview](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf)

---

## 8. DEFICIENCY CURE PROCESS

Per Section 3.4.4 of the LGIP, ISO-NE has **5 Business Days** to review submitted data. The Interconnection Customer then has up to **10 Business Days** to cure any data deficiencies, but **no later than the close of the Cluster Request Window**. [Source: ISO-NE Order 2023 FAQ, Jan. 2024](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/2024_01_18_tc_order2023_new_process_faq_final.pdf)

---

## 9. KEY GOVERNING DOCUMENTS

| Document | Description |
|---|---|
| **ISO-NE Tariff, Schedule 22 (LGIP)** | Core interconnection procedures ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf)) |
| **Appendix 1 to LGIP** | Interconnection Request form ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)) |
| **ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6** | Scope of interconnection studies ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/rules_proceds/isone_plan/pp05_6/pp5_6.pdf)) |
| **ISO Operating Procedure No. 14** | Technical requirements for generators |
| **ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide** | Process overview ([Web](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide)) |
| **Order No. 2023 Key Project Page** | Compliance documents, CRD forms, LC templates ([Web](https://iso-ne.com/committees/key-projects/order-no-2023-key-project)) |

---

## IMPORTANT CAVEATS

- The ISO Tariff governs in case of any discrepancy with the Process Guide or other ISO publications.
- The dollar figures cited reflect the **current post-Order No. 2023 compliance design** as reflected in ISO-NE's FERC-approved tariff revisions (effective August 12, 2024, with further revisions accepted August 28, 2025 in Docket ER24-2009-001).
- **Always consult the most current version of Schedule 22** on the ISO-NE OATT page and confirm details with IRTT Participant Support at **irtt@iso-ne.com** before submitting.
- The next Cluster Request Window (October 5 – November 19, 2026) is the first opportunity for new IR submissions after the transitional period.

I F I C A T I O N\n\nProposed Compliance Overview\n\n## Order 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\n##### Project Title: Order No. 2023 Compliance Proposed Effective Date: March 2024\n\n• ISO New England is preparing its compliance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) Order No. 2023 \n• This presentation provides: – A summary description of the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies \n• Order No. 2023 adopts most of the reforms proposed in the July 5, 2022 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NOPR”) \n\n### The Need for Reform\n\n• In Order No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, first-served study process – Cluster study process – Increased financial commitments for interconnection customers to enter and remain in the process – New mechanisms for interconnection customers to share interconnection study and upgrade costs – Enhanced site control requirements – Requirements for interconnection customers to select a definitive point of interconnection – Commercial readiness deposit requirements – Withdrawal penalties \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Increase the Speed of Interconnection Processing\n\n• Eliminates the reasonable efforts standard for completing interconnection studies, and establishes firm study deadlines along with financial penalties on transmission providers that fail to meet them \n• Adopts a uniform approach to Affected System coordination \n\n### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Incorporate Technological Advancements\n\n• Allow co-location of multiple generating facilities behind a single point of interconnection \n• Allow interconnection customers to access surplus interconnection once the original interconnection customer has an executed LGIA (or an unexecuted filed LGIA) \n• Allow interconnection customer-provided operating assumptions in interconnection studies that change the proposed charging behavior of an electric storage resource \n• Incorporate specific alternative transmission technologies for evaluation during the interconnection study process \n• Incorporate modeling and ride-through requirements for non synchronous generating facilities \n\nREVIEW OF ORDER REQUIREMENTS ISO’s Initial Proposed Compliance\n\n### Information Access\n\n##### Order No. 2023 Requirements ISO’s Proposed Approach\n\n• Feasibility Study is eliminated \n• Transmission providers to post and update the heatmap within 30 calendar days after the completion of each cluster study and cluster restudy. \n• Calculated under N-1 conditions and studied based on the power flow model of the transmission system (to approximate (C)NRIS) with specific technical requirements \n• FERC did not adopt the Informational Interconnection Study which was suggested in the NOPR \n• Comply with the heatmap requirement \n• The ISO will focus on compliance with the technically specific requirements for the initial implementation \n• ISO does not currently have significant mapping capability of this nature – will take some time to establish \n• Heatmap is not required until after the transition period – approximately mid 2025 \n\n### Cluster Study Process\n\n| 1. Cluster Request Window (45 Calendar Days (CD)) Interconnection Requests include: application, application fee, study deposit, commercial readiness deposit and demonstration of 90% site control, point of interconnection, CNRIS/NRIS election | | | |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| | | | |\n| 2. Cluster Engagement Window (60 CDs) Single Cluster Study Scoping meeting with all ICs Non-disclosure agreements Cluster Study Agreement | | | |\n| | | | |\n| 3. Cluster Study (270 CDs)* Power flow, short circuit, stability (& EMT analysis) | | | |\n| IRs are equally queued Non-binding cost estimates for network upgrades | | | Cluster Study Cost Allocation Network Upgrade Cost Allocation |\n| | | | |\n| 4. Cluster Restudy (if required) (150 CDs) Additional deposit for 5% of network upgrade cost | | | (see companion presentation on Order No. 2023 Transition for additional details) |\n| assignment from the Cluster Study Power flow, short circuit, stability, EMT analysis Updated network upgrade cost assignment | | | |\n\n*ISO will request a independent entity variation to allow 270 days for the completion of the Cluster Study\n\n### Cluster Study Process, continued\n\nOrder No. 2023 Requirements (see graphic on previous slide) ISO’s Proposed Approach\n\n• Cluster Request Window \n• Cluster Engagement Window \n• Cluster Study \n• Cluster Restudy \n• Adopt these changes with the following exceptions: \n• ISO will request 270 calendar days (instead of the Order’s 150 CDs) to complete the Cluster Study \n• Consistent with established timeline for System Impact Studies in New England \n• Establish a uniform study deposit amount of $250,000*, consistent with existing New England LGIP requirements \n• ISO anticipates opening the first Cluster Request Window in mid-2025 \n• After the completion of the Transitional Cluster Study \n\n*Independent entity variation Order No. 2023 proposes study deposits that vary with Generating Facility size\n\n### Cluster Study Process – ISO-NE Implementation\n\n• Single, region-wide cluster “window” – IRs for all portions of the region would be submitted and studied at the same time \n• However, IRs would be studied separately to the extent possible and would only be studied in gr",

      "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. However, you shouldn’t rely solely on this web guide. Consult the ISO New England Inc. Transmission, Markets, and Services Tariff(ISO Tariff), Section II, known as the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT), and relevant planning procedures and operating procedures to ensure that you are complying with all requirements. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window. The next window to submit a complete interconnection request package for the next Cluster Study is expected to be open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026. After the ISO validates your interconnection request and the cluster request window closes, the customer engagement window opens and a cluster study scoping meeting is held. A cluster study scoping meeting will address pertinent information about your project and the interconnection studies the ISO will perform to assess your project’s impact on the power system and the facilities and upgrades potentially needed to interconnect your project per all requirements and standards.\n\nAfter the ISO has completed the necessary study work, you’ll work with the ISO and the transmission owner on an Interconnection Agreement (IA), a formal document detailing the terms and conditions for interconnection service. You must enter into an IA before you can construct or operate a generator or an elective transmission upgrade. Final steps of the interconnection process are to submit final data and register your asset with the ISO.\n\n## Governing Documents\n\nSeveral schedules of the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff contain the rules detailing the interconnection process:\n\nSection II, ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT)\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n\n## Resources and Tools\n\n- ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6(PP 5-6), Scope of Study for System Impact Studies under the Generation Interconnection Procedures—describes the scope of interconnection studies conducted pursuant to Schedule 22, Schedule 23, and Schedule 25.\n- For additional resources for the Interconnection Process see Interconnection Service.\n- “Interconnection Process” training is available on the Training Materials webpage.\n- See the IRTT public queue. (Log in not necessary.) For information on the public version of the ISO New England Interconnection (ISO Queue), visit the Interconnection Request Queue webpage.\n- See the ISO Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT) and IRTT User Guide.\n- See the Interconnection Request Studies page to view completed studies for projects listed in the ISO’s Interconnection Request Queue (IR CEII required to view).\n- Interconnection frequently asked questions can be found in Ask ISO.\n- See Order No. 2023 Key Project page to access documents and resources related to the Order No. 2023: Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements.\n- Read ISO-NE’s September 30, 2025, responses to Order Nos. 2023/2023-A transition-related questions\n- Read the Interconnection Request Technical Data Submittal Guidance document for guidance on submitting the technical data required for a Cluster Study.\n\n## Questions\n\nEmail IRTT Participant Support at irtt@iso-ne.com, or contact ISO Participant Support.\n\n## Interconnection Documents\n\n### Filters\n\nFilter this list ↑\n\n### Documents\n\n| A listing of links to documents. | Title and Description | Date | Type | Size |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n\nSorry, no results are available with this choice of filter(s).       "text": "D E C E M B E R 2 1 , 2 0 2 3 | N E P O O L T R A N S M I S S I O N C O M M I T T E E | D O U B L E T R E E H O T E L , W E S T B O R O U G H , M A\n\nAl McBride D I R E C T O R | T R A N S M I S S I O N S E R V I C E S & R E S O U R C E Q U A L I F I C A T I O N\n\n##### Summary of Tariff Redline Design Details\n\n## Order 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\n### Presentation Overview\n\n• ISO has posted the initial draft of all Tariff redlines associated with Order No. 2023 compliance \n• This presentation summarizes the key components of the compliance design and responds to several Stakeholder comments and questions \n• Focus on Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP) in Schedule 22 of the Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) – The Small Generator Interconnection Procedures (SGIP) in Schedule 23 of the OATT have been overhauled to mirror the LGIP \n• The pre-application process has been maintained in the SGIP – The Elective Transmission Upgrade Interconnection Procedures (ETUIP) in Schedule 25 of the OATT also closely match the LGIP \n\n### ACCEPTABLE FORM OF READINESS DEPOSITS\n\n### Commercial Readiness Deposit\n\nTariff Section Tariff Change Reason for Change\n\nLGIP\n\nCommercial Readiness Deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer or through the provision and maintenance of a Letter of Credit in a form and from a financial institution acceptable to System Operator, as described on the System Operator’s public website. All other deposits that must be submitted to the System Operator under this LGIP must be paid in cash and delivered to the System Operator’s bank account by electronic transfer within the period specified in the respective provision. A deposit will not be considered received until it is in the System Operator’s bank account or, in the case of a Letter of Credit provided as a Commercial Readiness Deposit, the Letter of Credit is accepted by System Operator. the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, first-served study process – Cluster study process – Increased financial commitments for interconnection customers to enter and remain in the process – New mechanisms for interconnection customers to share interconnection study and upgrade costs – Enhanced site control requirements – Requirements for interconnection customers to select a definitive point of interconnection – Commercial readiness deposit requirements – Withdrawal penalties \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Increase the Speed of Interconnection Processing\n\n• Eliminates the reasonable efforts standard for completing interconnection studies, and establishes firm study deadlines along with financial penalties on transmission providers that fail to meet them \n• Adopts a uniform approach to Affected System coordination \n\n### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Incorporate Technological Advancements\n\n• Allow co-location of multiple generating facilities behind a single point of interconnection \n• Allow interconnection customers to access surplus interconnection once the original interconnection customer has an executed LGIA (or an unexecuted filed LGIA) \n• Allow interconnection customer-provided operating assumptions in interconnection studies that change the proposed charging behavior of an electric storage resource \n• Incorporate specific alternative transmission technologies for evaluation during the interconnection study process \n• Incorporate modeling and ride-through requirements for non synchronous generating facilities \n\nREVIEW OF ORDER       "text": "Interconnection Process Guide\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/getSignInInfo\n\n/isoexpress/webservices/-/webservice/authenticate\n\n# Interconnection Process Guide\n\n### In this section\n\n- Interconnection Process Guide\n- Is the ISO New England Interconnection Process Right for You?\n- Register for an IRTT Account\n- Submit an Interconnection Request During Cluster Request Window\n- Register Your Asset\n\n### Related Links\n\n- Glossary of Interconnection-Related Acronyms\n- Interconnection-Related Governing Documents\n- Contact IRTT Support\n\nISO New England is providing this web guide to assist you in understanding the interconnection process for small and large generating facilities and elective transmission upgrades (ETUs) to interconnect to the administered transmission system. In case of any discrepancy with this web guide, the ISO Tariff and applicable operating procedures and planning procedures govern.\n\n## Overview of the Interconnection Process\n\nAs a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO), the ISO coordinates how transmission lines, power plants, and other resources connect to and operate on New England’s administered transmission system to ensure reliability and facilitate wholesale electricity market participation. The ISO does not administer the interconnection process for projects subject to state interconnection processes. Thus, after you determine that the ISO interconnection process applies to your project, you’ll need to submit an interconnection request to the ISO through the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool(IRTT). Existing customers can register for an account using IRTT, while new customers must first or simultaneously obtain an ISO customer ID and access to critical energy infrastructure information.\n\nAs required by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Order No. 2023, it is only possible to submit interconnection requests during a cluster request window. The next window to submit a complete interconnection request package for the next Cluster Study is expected to be open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026. After the ISO validates your interconnection request and the cluster request window closes, the customer engagement window opens and a cluster study scoping meeting is held. A cluster study scoping meeting will address pertinent information about your project and the interconnection studies the ISO will perform to assess your project’s impact on the power system and the facilities and upgrades potentially needed to interconnect your project per all requirements and standards.\n\nAfter the ISO has completed the necessary study work, you’ll work with the ISO and the transmission owner on an Interconnection Agreement (IA), a formal document detailing the terms and conditions for interconnection service. Final steps of the interconnection process are to submit final data and register your asset with the ISO.\n\n## Governing Documents\n\nSeveral schedules of the ISO’s Open Access Transmission Tariff contain the rules detailing the interconnection process:\n\nSection II, ISO New England Open Access Transmission Tariff(OATT)\n\n- Helps ensure that all transmission owners and transmission customers have open access to transmission service in New England\n- Details the rights and responsibilities of transmission owners and transmission customers as well as the procedures they must follow and the fees transmission customers must pay to access the transmission system\n- Sets out the transmission system planning process for the region\n\n## Resources and Tools\n\n- ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6(PP 5-6), Scope of Study for System Impact Studies under the Generation Interconnection Procedures—describes the scope of interconnection studies conducted pursuant to Schedule 22, Schedule 23, and Schedule 25.\n- For additional resources for the Interconnection Process see Interconnection Service.\n- “Interconnection Process” training is available on the Training Materials webpage.\n- See the IRTT public queue. steps.content:
# ISO New England — Valid Interconnection Application Requirements & Fees for Large Generators (>20 MW)

Based on the latest ISO-NE Schedule 22 (Large Generator Interconnection Procedures, effective May 3, 2025, per ER25-2149-000), as reformed by FERC Order No. 2023 and Order No. 2023-A, the following summarizes the requirements and fees to submit a valid Interconnection Request (IR) for a large generating facility.

---

## 1. THRESHOLD ELIGIBILITY

Schedule 22 applies to **Large Generating Facilities (>20 MW)** interconnecting to the ISO-NE Administered Transmission System. [Source: ISO-NE OATT page](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/rules-procedures/oatt)

---

## 2. PRE-APPLICATION PREREQUISITES

Before submitting an IR, the Interconnection Customer must:

- Obtain an **ISO Customer ID**
- Obtain **Critical Energy Infrastructure Information (CEII) clearance**
- Register for an account in the **Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT)**

[Source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide/submit-an-interconnection-request)

---

## 3. TIMING: CLUSTER REQUEST WINDOW

Under Order No. 2023, IRs may **only** be submitted during a **45-Calendar-Day Cluster Request Window**. All IRs submitted and validated within the same window are considered **equally queued**. [Source: ISO-NE Order 2023 Compliance Overview, Sept. 2023](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf)

The **next Cluster Request Window** is expected to open **October 5, 2026 through November 19, 2026**. [Source: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide)

---

## 4. COMPLETE INTERCONNECTION REQUEST PACKAGE (Section 3.4.2 of the LGIP)

To be deemed a **valid Interconnection Request**, the following must be submitted during the Cluster Request Window:

### 4A. Appendix 1 Interconnection Request Form

The completed form (Appendix 1 to the LGIP) must include:

| Element | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Project type** | New Large Generating Facility, Material Modification, or NR→CNR change |
| **Interconnection Service election** | Network Resource (NRIS — energy only) or Capacity Network Resource (CNRIS — energy + capacity) |
| **Facility location** | Address, Town/City, County, State |
| **Requested Point of Interconnection (POI)** | Definitive POI required |
| **Generating Facility type & fuel** | Including electric storage specifications if applicable |
| **Capacity data** | Gross/Net MW at four temperature benchmarks (≥0°F, ≥20°F, ≥50°F, ≥90°F) |
| **Requested service levels (MW)** | CNR Capability Summer/Winter; NR Capability Summer/Winter |
| **Equipment configuration** | Number of units, GSUs, control technologies |
| **Key dates** | Commercial Operation Date, Initial Synchronization Date, In-Service Date |
| **Customer information** | Company name, ISO Customer ID#, authorized signature |

[Source: ISO-NE Appendix 1 Interconnection Request form](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)

### 4B. Technical Data & Models

All technical data, models (PSS/E, EMT as applicable), and attachments specified in Appendix 1 must be submitted with the IR. ISO recommends submitting **at least 15 business days before the close of the CRW** to allow time for review and curing of deficiencies. [Source: ISO-NE Order 2023 FAQ, Jan. 2024](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/2024_01_18_tc_order2023_new_process_faq_final.pdf)

### 4C. Site Control

| Service Type | Requirement |
|---|---|
| **CNR Interconnection Service** | **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name |
| **NR Interconnection Service** | **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name; **OR, in lieu thereof:** (i) a **$10,000/MW deposit** (min $500,000 / max $2,000,000), (ii) a signed officer's affidavit stating Site Control is unobtainable due to Regulatory Limitations, and (iii) documentation explaining the Regulatory Limitations |
| **Modification to existing facility** | Customer certifies it already has Site Control and no additional real property is required |

*Note: The Order No. 2023 compliance design references 90% site control at the IR stage (per the presentation slides), while the currently posted Appendix 1 form continues to reference 100% for CNR. The tariff itself (Section 3.4.2) as of the latest LGIP effective date (May 3, 2025) should be consulted for the binding requirement.*

[Source: Appendix 1 form; ISO-NE Dec. 2023 TC presentation](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100006/a06_12212023_order_2023_compliance_december_tc_summary_presentation.pdf)

---

## 5. FEES & DEPOSITS — SUMMARY TABLE

All deposits to the System Operator must be paid in **cash via electronic transfer** (study deposits and initial deposit), while the Commercial Readiness Deposit may be in the form of **cash or an irrevocable Letter of Credit** from an ISO-NE-approved financial institution. [Source: ISO-NE Dec. 2023 TC presentation](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100006/a06_12212023_order_2023_compliance_december_tc_summary_presentation.pdf)

| Fee/Deposit | Amount (New NR/CNR) | Amount (NR→CNR Change) | Refundable? | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Deposit** | **$50,000** | **$50,000** | Potentially non-refundable | Applied toward ISO costs for processing the IR, developing study agreements, and developing the LGIA |
| **Study Deposit** | **$250,000** | **$100,000** | Refundable (unspent portion) | Applied toward Cluster Study costs |
| **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)** | **$500,000** (2× study deposit) | **$200,000** (2× study deposit) | Refunded per Section 3.7 upon meeting milestones | Demonstrates commercial readiness |
| **Site Control Alternative Deposit** (NR only) | **$10,000/MW** (min $500K, max $2M) | N/A | Refundable within cure period | In lieu of Site Control for NR, when Regulatory Limitations exist |

**Total upfront at IR submission (new CNR/NR): $800,000** (initial $50K + study $250K + CRD $500K)

[Source: ISO-NE Jan. 2024 TC presentation, LGIP Section 3.4.1.1 redlines](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/a03.1b_2024_01_23_tc_order2023_compliance_presentation.pdf)

---

## 6. SUBSEQUENT FINANCIAL COMMITMENTS THROUGH THE PROCESS

| Stage | Trigger | Financial Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Post-Cluster Study Report** | Within 20 CD of Cluster Study Report Meeting | Additional CRD = **5% of assigned Network Upgrade costs** |
| **Facilities Study Entry** | Execution of Facilities Study Agreement | CRD = **10% of Network Upgrade costs**, plus **greater of 25% of estimated FS cost or $250,000**, plus proof of **100% Site Control** |
| **LGIA Execution** | Upon signing the Interconnection Agreement | **20% deposit** (less readiness deposits already on hand) |

[Sources: ISO-NE Oct. 2023 Cluster Study Cycle presentation; Appendix 3 (Facilities Study Agreement)](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100004/a03b_2023_10_17_tc_order2023_proposed_compliance_overview.pdf)

---

## 7. WITHDRAWAL PENALTIES

Under Order No. 2023, withdrawal penalties apply if an Interconnection Customer withdraws after certain milestones. The Commercial Readiness Deposit is at risk if the customer withdraws without meeting the applicable milestone. [Source: ISO-NE Order 2023 Compliance Overview](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf)

---

## 8. DEFICIENCY CURE PROCESS

Per Section 3.4.4 of the LGIP, ISO-NE has **5 Business Days** to review submitted data. The Interconnection Customer then has up to **10 Business Days** to cure any data deficiencies, but **no later than the close of the Cluster Request Window**. [Source: ISO-NE Order 2023 FAQ, Jan. 2024](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/2024_01_18_tc_order2023_new_process_faq_final.pdf)

---

## 9. KEY GOVERNING DOCUMENTS

| Document | Description |
|---|---|
| **ISO-NE Tariff, Schedule 22 (LGIP)** | Core interconnection procedures ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf)) |
| **Appendix 1 to LGIP** | Interconnection Request form ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)) |
| **ISO Planning Procedure No. 5-6** | Scope of interconnection studies ([PDF](https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/rules_proceds/isone_plan/pp05_6/pp5_6.pdf)) |
| **ISO Operating Procedure No. 14** | Technical requirements for generators |
| **ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide** | Process overview ([Web](https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide)) |
| **Order No. 2023 Key Project Page** | Compliance documents, CRD forms, LC templates ([Web](https://iso-ne.com/committees/key-projects/order-no-2023-key-project)) |

---

## IMPORTANT CAVEATS

- The ISO Tariff governs in case of any discrepancy with the Process Guide or other ISO publications.
- The dollar figures cited reflect the **current post-Order No. 2023 compliance design** as reflected in ISO-NE's FERC-approved tariff revisions (effective August 12, 2024, with further revisions accepted August 28, 2025 in Docket ER24-2009-001).
- **Always consult the most current version of Schedule 22** on the ISO-NE OATT page and confirm details with IRTT Participant Support at **irtt@iso-ne.com** before submitting.
- The next Cluster Request Window (October 5 – November 19, 2026) is the first opportunity for new IR submissions after the transitional period.
